steps.title: An LED that listens to you

narration.default: Every doorbell, elevator button, and keyboard key on your laptop does one tiny thing: it senses a press and switches something else on. Today you'll teach an LED that exact trick. Press the button — the light comes on. Let go — the light goes off. Before you start, make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode. If you haven't done that yet, finish the Blink an LED tutorial first.

Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. Just like Scratch has 'when green flag clicked' and 'forever', Arduino has setup and loop. Setup runs once when the program starts. Loop runs forever, over and over, while the Arduino has power.

An Arduino has rows of labelled holes around the edge of the board — those are the pins. Each pin is a wire connection point. Some pins send voltage out (to lights, motors). Some pins listen for voltage coming in (from buttons, sensors). The Arduino needs to know which is which. Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 13 and mode to output — pin 13 will be sending voltage out to the LED.

Drag a second pinMode block below the first. Set pin to 2 and change mode to input. input is the opposite of output — instead of sending voltage out, pin 2 will listen for voltage coming in. That's how the Arduino will know when you've pressed the button.

There's a problem with plain input: when a pin is connected to nothing, the reading flips between high and low randomly. The Arduino can't tell what's really happening on that pin. Click the mode dropdown and change it to input pullup. This tells the Arduino to quietly hold pin 2 at high (5 volts) all on its own, until something specific changes that. Now the readings are stable.

We want the LED to react differently when the button is pressed vs not pressed. That's exactly what an if-else block is for — same block you've seen in Scratch. Open the Control category and drag an if-else block into the loop section. Whatever question we put in its diamond slot will run the if branch when the answer is yes, and the else branch when the answer is no.

steps.title: Drop equals into the diamond slot

narration.default: Open the Operators category. Drag the equals block. Drop it into the diamond slot of the if-else — that's where the yes-or-no question goes. The equals block compares two things; we'll fill in what to compare in the next two steps.

steps.title: Drop digitalRead into the left side

narration.default: Back to Arduino. Drag a digitalRead block. Drop it into the left slot of the equals block. Set its pin to 2. digitalRead is how a program senses the world — it checks what voltage is sitting on a pin right now and reports back high (5 volts) or low (0 volts). It doesn't change anything; it just looks.

steps.title: Drop low into the right side

narration.default: Still in the Arduino category, drag a low block. Drop it into the right slot of the equals block. Your question now reads: 'is pin 2 low right now?' Or in plain words: 'is pin 2 at 0 volts right now?'

Strange, right? Pressing should mean on, not off. Here's why. input pullup holds pin 2 at high (5 volts) all the time. The button is wired so pressing connects pin 2 to a special wire that's at 0 volts. That pulls pin 2's reading down from 5 volts to 0 volts — which is low. So pressed = low. We'll meet that 0-volt wire when we wire up the button.

We've used digitalRead to listen. Now meet its twin: digitalWrite — the block that lets your program control things. digitalWrite takes two inputs: a pin number and a value (high or low). When you set a pin high, the Arduino puts 5 volts on it. When you set it low, 0 volts. That's it. Connect an LED, a buzzer, a motor driver, or even another Arduino — digitalWrite is how your program turns them on and off. We'll drop two of these next: one for when the button is pressed, one for when it isn't.

Drag a digitalWrite block into the if branch — the top half of the if-else. Set pin to 13 and value to high. When the if-question's answer is yes (button pressed), this line runs: 5 volts on pin 13, LED lights up.

Drag another digitalWrite into the else branch — the bottom half. Pin 13, value low. When the question's answer is no (button not pressed), this line runs instead: 0 volts on pin 13, LED dark. The blocks are done — now let's wire up the simulator.

Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose LED. In the pin dialog, choose pin 13 and click Save. The virtual LED appears next to the virtual Arduino — wired to the same pin your code is writing high and low to.

steps.title: Add a push button on pin 2

narration.default: Click the plus button again, choose Push Button, set pin to 2, click Save. The button is now wired between pin 2 and… well, that's exactly what the next step is about.

A battery has two terminals — positive (+) and negative (−). On an Arduino, the negative terminal is called ground, often labelled GND on the board. Ground is just a name for 0 volts. Now we can tell the full story of our button. When the button is not pressed, input pullup is gently holding pin 2 at 5 volts — so digitalRead reports high. When you press the button, it connects pin 2 directly to ground. That direct connection beats the gentle pullup, so pin 2 drops to 0 volts — and digitalRead reports low. that'S why pressed = low and not-pressed = high. The mystery from the question we built earlier is solved.
Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The simulator goes live.

Click and hold the simulated button. The LED lights up. Let go — it goes dark. You just built a real-world reactive program. The same trick is inside every doorbell, every light switch, every keyboard key, every elevator button you've ever pressed.

Try this: swap high and low in the two digitalWrite blocks. Now pressing turns the LED off, and letting go turns it on — a backwards lamp. Or add a second LED on pin 12, give it its own pinMode and digitalWrites, and make it light up only when the button is released. Two LEDs, opposite reactions, same button.

Five minutes ago you didn't know any of this. Now you do: pinMode tells the Arduino which pins send voltage and which listen for it. digitalWrite tells a pin to be high (5 volts) or low (0 volts) — that's how programs control the world. digitalRead asks a pin what voltage is on it right now — that's how programs sense the world. if-else lets your program decide between two paths — same as Scratch. ground, often labelled GND, is the name for the 0-volt wire — the reference point for everything else. Programs that read the world, decide, and react are everywhere — alarms, traffic lights, smart locks, your phone's buttons. You can write them now.
